mainmatter.tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f
mainmatter.p: For any f \colon A \to B, it is evident that the type of inverses to f is (up to reassociation) precisely the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f }.

frontmatter.title: Coherent remainder as a path object

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a function equipped with a section \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {}. We can equip the coherent remainder of \sigma with the structure of a path object as follows:

ol.li: We equip g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {}

For each \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, we likewise equip \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}

li.p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0, \eta _1 \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } and \eta _{01} \colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^* \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _1 \sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _0 \sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

mainmatter.p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}
mainmatter.p: Given elements \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _0, \chi _0 \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _1, \chi _1 \right ) \mathclose {} of \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma, the resulting type \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _0, \chi _0 \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _1, \chi _1 \right ) \mathclose {} is definitionally equivalent to the following dependent sum:
mainmatter.tex: \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _{01}: \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _{01}: \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _{01}: \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _{01}: \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \chi _0 \sim \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {act}_{ \mathsf {act}_{ f } } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _{01}x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot \chi _1 x

frontmatter.title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

mainmatter.p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {} be a section of a function f \colon A \to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} P &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ P & \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } } \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.
For any function f \colon A \to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

Without loss of generality, we may assume that f has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon , \eta , \chi \right ) \mathclose {}. By , it suffices to argue that the type of inverses to f is a retract of a proposition; the type of inverses to f is in fact equivalent to the type of sections equipped with a coherent remainder:
mainmatter.tex: \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \sigma : \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \sigma : \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \sigma : \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \sigma : \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma
mainmatter.p: By , it suffices to show that the type of sections to f is a proposition and that for every such section, the coherent remainder thereof is a proposition. The latter is , and the former follows from by means of the retraction \mathopen {} \left ( g, \eta \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Being an embedding is a proposition

mainmatter.p: That a given function f \colon A \to B is an embedding is a proposition.
By and .
That a given map f \colon A \to B is bijective is a proposition.
By , via .
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

ol.li: a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert of objects, written c \in C;
for each x,y \in C a set C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} of arrows, written f \colon x \to y;
for each x \in C, an identity arrow 1_{ x } \colon x \to x;
for each f \colon x \to y and g \colon y \to z, a composite arrow g \circ f \colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows: \boldsymbol { \lambda }_{ f } \colon 1_{ y } \circ f = f \quad \boldsymbol { \rho }_{ f } \colon f \circ 1_{ x } = f \quad \boldsymbol { \alpha }_{ f , g , h } \colon \mathopen {} \left ( h \circ g \right ) \mathclose {} \circ f = h \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {}

mainmatter.p: A functor F \colon C \to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

ol.li: a function \left \lvert F \right \rvert \colon \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert D \right \rvert giving the action of F on objects, where we shall write Fx for \left \lvert F \right \rvert x given x \in C;

for each x,y \in C a function F_{x,y} \colon C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \to D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Fy \right ) \mathclose {} giving the action of F on arrows, where we shall write Ff \colon Fx \to Fy for F_{x,y}f given f \colon x \to y;

such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions: F^{ 1_{ x } } \colon F 1_{ x } = 1_{ Fx } \quad F^{g \circ f} \colon F \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = Fg \circ Ff

mainmatter.p: A functor F \colon C \to D is called (full, faithful) when the action on arrows is valued in (surjections, embeddings) respectively. It is common to refer to a full and faithful functor as fully faithful.
Fullness and faithfulness are both properties of a functor.
A functor F \colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f \colon C \cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } } {D \cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D &: \mathsf {Precategory} \\ F &: D \cong C \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \to C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \to C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible} \, F_0 \\ h_2 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \cong C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda x,y \mathpunct {.} { C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.

Let C and D be precategories, and let F,G \colon C \to D be two functors from C to D. A natural transformation \alpha \colon F \to G is given by a family of arrows \alpha _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } } D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Gx \right ) \mathclose {} equipped with a (necessarily unique) naturality datum \alpha _f \colon \alpha _y \circ Ff = Gf \circ \alpha _x for each f \colon x \to y.
A section-retraction pair in a precategory C consists of an arrow s \colon x \to y called the section and an arrow r \colon y \to x called the retraction such that r \circ s = 1_{ x }.

An arrow f \colon x \to y in a precategory C is called an isomorphism when either of there exists an inverse to f, i.e. an arrow g \colon y \to x that is simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
We shall write f \colon x \cong y to denote an isomorphism from x to y.

A precategory C is called univalent when for any x \in C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } y \cong x of isomorphisms onto x is contractible.
A category is defined to be a univalent precategory.
The material on univalent category theory is drawn primarily from the HoTT Book and Ahrens, Kapulkin, and Shulman.
A displayed precategory C over a precategory B, written B \vdash C, is specified by the following data:

ol.li: for each b \in B, a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert b of displayed objects over b that we shall write b.c \in C;
for each b_0.c_0, b_1.c_1 \in C and i \colon b_0 \to b_1, a set C _{ i } \mathopen {} \left ( b_0.c_0 , b_1.c_1 \right ) \mathclose {} of displayed arrows over i;
for each b.c \in C a displayed identity arrow 1_{ b } . 1_{ c } \colon b.c \to b.c;

for each i.f \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1 and j.g \colon b_1.c_1 \to b_2.c_2, a displayed composite arrow \mathopen {} \left ( j \circ i \right ) \mathclose {} \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1;
such that composition is associative and unital with respect to displayed identity arrows.

mainmatter.p: Given two displayed objects b.c,b.c' in a displayed precategory B \vdash C, we shall refer to displayed arrows of the form 1_{ b } .f \colon b.c \to b.c' as vertical.

Let B \vdash C be a displayed precategory. We define the fiber of C over b \in B to be the precategory b.C whose objects are given by displayed objects b.c \in C and whose arrows b.c_0 \to b.c_1 are given by vertical displayed arrows 1_{ b } .f \colon b.c_0 \to b.c_1, with identities and compositions inherited from C.
A displayed precategory B \vdash C is called univalent when each of its fiber precategories b.C is univalent.
A displayed category is defined to be a univalent displayed precategory.

The total (pre)category B.C of a displayed precategory B \vdash C is defined so that \left \lvert B.C \right \rvert is the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b \in B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b \in B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b \in B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b \in B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \left \lvert C \right \rvert b of displayed objects of C and B.C \mathopen {} \left ( b.c , b'.c' \right ) \mathclose {} is the type of displayed morphisms i.f \colon b.c \to b'.c' in C, with identities and compositions inherited from C.

Given a displayed precategory B \vdash C, we may define a functor \pi _{ B \vdash C } \colon B.C \to B called the display functor, sending b.c \in B.C to b and i.f \colon b.c \to b'.c' to i \colon b \to b'.

frontmatter.title: Conditions for the total precategory to be univalent

mainmatter.p: Let B \vdash C be a precategory displayed over a precategory B. If B is univalent and B \vdash C is univalent, then the total precategory B.C is univalent.

frontmatter.title: Univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

mainmatter.p: Give an example of a displayed category B \vdash C over a precategory B such that the total precategory B.C is univalent but B is nonetheless not univalent.
Let B \vdash C,D be two displayed precategories. A vertical displayed functor from C to D over B, written B.F \colon C \to D, is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each displayed object b.c \in C, a displayed object b.Fc \in D;
for each displayed arrow i.f \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1 in C, a displayed arrow i.Ff \colon b_0.Fc_0 \to b_1.Fc_1 in D;
such the assignment of displayed arrows preserves displayed identities and displayed composites.

mainmatter.p: Let B.F, B.G \colon C \to D be two vertical displayed functors between two displayed precategories B \vdash C,D. A vertical displayed natural transformation from B.F to B.G over B, written B. \alpha \colon B.F \to B.G, is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each displayed object b.c \in C, a vertical arrow b. \alpha _c \colon b.Fc \to b.Gc;

mainmatter.p: The material on displayed categories is drawn from Ahrens and Lumsdaine, although we have imposed our own terminological and notational conventions.

The purpose of displayed categories is to define category-like objects that depend on other categories. We will later on learn how to impose conditions that make displayed categories behave like (potentially large) categories internal to other categories. In order to make the theory sufficiently flexible, however, it is necessary to first relativise the notion of displayed category to allow the base to be another (relative) displayed category rather than an ordinary category. The starting point of our approach to relativisation is the insight that a category is a special case of displayed category.
A displayed category over the terminal category gives precisely the data of a category.

In light of the observation above, the base of a displayed category in the sense that we have described so far is another displayed category — whose base is the terminal category. This suggests a simple way to expand our vocabulary to account for displayed categories whose base is another displayed category.

Let B \vdash C displayed precategory over a precategory B. A relative displayed precategory over C, written C \vdash E, is defined to be a displayed precategory B.C \vdash E over the total precategory B.C. A relative displayed category over C is then a displayed category over B.C.
When it is clear from context what we mean, we will speak of displayed categories over C, as in what follows, the relative version of displayed categories will be the primary one.

frontmatter.title: The relative displayed precategory of slices

mainmatter.p: Let B \vdash C be a displayed precategory; we may define a relative displayed precategory C \vdash C _{/} as follows:

ol.li: A displayed object b.c.p \in C _{/} is given by a vertical arrow over b \in B with codomain b.c, i.e. an arrow p \colon \partial _0 p \to c in the fiber precategory b.C.

frontmatter.title: Displayed categories
frontmatter.meta: @article{ahrens-lumsdaine-2019, author = {Ahrens, Benedikt and Lumsdaine, Peter LeFanu}, url = {https://lmcs.episciences.org/5252}, year = {2019}, month = mar, doi = {10.23638/LMCS-15(1:20)2019}, eprint = {1705.04296}, eprinttype = {arXiv}, issue = {1}, journal = {Logical Methods in Computer Science}, title = {Displayed Categories}, volume = {15}, }

mainmatter.p: We introduce and develop the notion of displayed categories. A displayed category over a category C is equivalent to “a category D and functor F : D \to C, but instead of having a single collection of “objects of D” with a map to the objects of C, the objects are given as a family indexed by objects of C, and similarly for the morphisms. This encapsulates a common way of building categories in practice, by starting with an existing category and adding extra data/properties to the objects and morphisms. The interest of this seemingly trivial reformulation is that various properties of functors are more naturally defined as properties of the corresponding displayed categories. Grothendieck fibrations, for example, when defined as certain functors, use equality on objects in their definition. When defined instead as certain displayed categories, no reference to equality on objects is required. Moreover, almost all examples of fibrations in nature are, in fact, categories whose standard construction can be seen as going via displayed categories. We therefore propose displayed categories as a basis for the development of fibrations in the type-theoretic setting, and similarly for various other notions whose classical definitions involve equality on objects. Besides giving a conceptual clarification of such issues, displayed categories also provide a powerful tool in computer formalisation, unifying and abstracting common constructions and proof techniques of category theory, and enabling modular reasoning about categories of multi-component structures. As such, most of the material of this article has been formalised in Coq over the UniMath library, with the aim of providing a practical library for use in further developments.

frontmatter.title: Univalent categories and the Rezk completion
frontmatter.meta: @article{ahrens-kapulkin-shulman-2015, title={Univalent categories and the Rezk completion}, volume={25}, doi={10.1017/S0960129514000486}, number={5}, journal={Mathematical Structures in Computer Science}, publisher={Cambridge University Press}, author={Ahrens, Benedikt and Kapulkin, Krzysztof and Shulman, Michael}, year={2015}, pages={1010–1039} }

frontmatter.title: Homotopy Type Theory: univalent foundations of mathematics

meta.name: venue

link.title: IAS Special Year on Univalent Foundations

meta.link: IAS Special Year on Univalent Foundations

frontmatter.meta: @book{hottbook, author = {{Univalent Foundations Program}, The}, address = {Institute for Advanced Study}, publisher = {\url{https://homotopytypetheory.org/book}}, year = {2013}, title = {Homotopy Type Theory: Univalent Foundations of Mathematics}, }

mainmatter.p: Homotopy type theory is a new branch of mathematics that combines aspects of several different fields in a surprising way. It is based on a recently discovered connection between homotopy theory and type theory. It touches on topics as seemingly distant as the homotopy groups of spheres, the algorithms for type checking, and the definition of weak \infty-groupoids. Homotopy type theory offers a new “univalent” foundation of mathematics, in which a central role is played by Voevodsky’s univalence axiom and higher inductive types. The present book is intended as a first systematic exposition of the basics of univalent foundations, and a collection of examples of this new style of reasoning — but without requiring the reader to know or learn any formal logic, or to use any computer proof assistant. We believe that univalent foundations will eventually become a viable alternative to set theory as the “implicit foundation” for the unformalized mathematics done by most mathematicians.
